analysis_steps: Step 1: Noted all peaks are in ultra-low-frequency region (<50 cm⁻¹), ruling out most common materials (Si, graphene, MoS2 high-freq modes).
Step 2: The spectral window is only 72 cm⁻¹ wide, meaning only rigid-layer and low-energy phonon modes are captured.
Step 3: The dominant peak at 6.74 cm⁻¹ with very high intensity points to an interlayer shear/breathing mode of a layered material with heavy constituent atoms.
Step 4: The peak at 43.67 cm⁻¹ is consistent with the lowest optical phonon (E_g) of Bi2Se3 or related tetradymite-type materials.
Step 5: Compared peak positions with known ultra-low-frequency modes of Bi2Se3 (~7, ~14, ~37–40 cm⁻¹ interlayer modes), finding reasonable agreement.
Step 6: Bi2Te3 and Sb2Te3 considered as alternatives since they share the same crystal structure, but Bi2Se3 modes best match the observed shifts.
Step 7: Sharp linewidths and low noise confirm high crystalline quality consistent with bulk single-crystal characterization.
Step 8: Assigned bulk layer number since interlayer breathing/shear modes are well-resolved, indicating multiple quintuple layers.
decision_trace: All peaks below 50 cm⁻¹ indicating ultra-low-frequency lattice modes of heavy-atom material
Spectral range covers only 72 cm⁻¹, so only interlayer/rigid-layer modes are captured
Dominant peak at 6.74 cm⁻¹ consistent with interlayer shear mode of layered chalcogenide
Peak at 43.67 cm⁻¹ near expected E_g mode of Bi2Se3 (~37 cm⁻¹, shifted)
Sharp peaks (FWHM ~0.5–2 cm⁻¹) indicate high crystalline quality
High peak density (307 rough peaks) suggests rich phonon structure typical of layered crystal
